items.base: Replace the linear warmup ramp with a polynomial ramp whose exponent p is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, so the learning rate rises as (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w. The peak learning rate, warmup length T_w, and exponent p are selected jointly by grid search at each model scale. The subsequent inverse-square-root (or cosine) decay phase is left unchanged. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
items.padded: Borrowing the language of driven non-equilibrium systems, treat the warmup phase as a quench protocol that ramps a control field (the learning rate) from a quiescent zero state toward its operating point. Rather than the conventional constant-slope (linear) drive, impose a power-law ramp in which the learning rate follows (t/T_w)^p across a warmup window of duration T_w, so that p plays the role of a tunable scaling exponent governing how sharply the system approaches its steady drive — a knob directly analogous to the critical exponent controlling a power-law approach to a critical point in the theory of phase transitions. The exponent p, the window T_w, and the peak (operating) learning rate are co-optimized by a grid sweep at each model scale, treated as the three coordinates parameterizing the quench. The post-warmup relaxation stage (inverse-square-root or cosine decay) is left as-is. The protocol is assessed by comparing steady-state validation perplexity and the transient stability of the training trajectory against the linear-drive baseline across several model sizes.

items.base: Introduce a separate schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so that the realized per-step regularization is controlled independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized against a reference value, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate schedule no longer scales the effective weight decay; the coefficient may then follow its own tuned schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are unchanged � only the weight-decay term's dependence on the learning rate is removed. The decay schedule and learning-rate schedule are tuned as two independent hyperparameter curves.
items.padded: Cast the optimizer as a driven dissipative dynamical system in which the adaptive update supplies the drive and weight decay acts as a linear damping (dissipation) term. In the usual coupling this damping is modulated by the same time-varying gain as the drive — the learning rate — so any annealing of the gain inadvertently detunes the realized dissipation. Introduce a gain-normalization step in the spirit of feedback linearization from control theory: divide the decay term by the instantaneous learning rate and normalize it against a fixed reference gain, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate trajectory no longer scales the effective damping. Freed from this coupling, the damping coefficient can then track its own homeostatic set-point schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state — the internal drive dynamics — remain untouched; only the learning-rate dependence of the dissipation term is removed. The damping (weight-decay) schedule and the gain (learning-rate) schedule are subsequently tuned as two independent control curves.

items.base: Extend Lion by storing its single momentum buffer in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format (for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors) rather than 16- or 32-bit floating point. The momentum is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized after each step, so the sign-based update rule is unchanged and only the storage representation of the state differs. This reduces Lion's remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half, and because the update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, moderate quantization error on the buffer is expected to have limited effect on the update direction. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.
items.padded: View Lion's momentum buffer through the lens of lossy source coding: it is a stored signal whose sole functional readout is the polarity of an interpolation, so under a rate-distortion analysis most of its bit-precision is redundant channel capacity. Encode the buffer with a block-wise scalar quantizer — for example 8-bit codewords sharing a per-block gain (scaling) factor — in place of a 16- or 32-bit floating-point representation. On each step the codeword is decoded back to a real value to form the sign-momentum update and re-encoded afterward, so the update rule (the actual computation) is bit-for-bit identical and only the channel representation of the state changes. This roughly halves Lion's residual optimizer-state memory. Because the update extracts only the sign — the polarity bit — of the interpolated momentum, quantization noise injected into the lower-order bits is expected to leave the decision boundary, and hence the update direction, largely intact, much as a threshold/sign code is robust to sub-threshold channel perturbations. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.

items.base: Apply the whitening transformation to embeddings produced by a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder, and replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient is selected on held-out data to stabilize the whitening matrix when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
items.padded: Frame the geometry problem as one of efficient sensory coding: an anisotropic embedding cloud is a redundant, correlated code, and 'sphering' it — the decorrelating whitening long studied for retinal and early-visual representations — is precisely the transform that removes second-order redundancy and maximizes isotropy. Apply this sphering operation to the embeddings of a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder. Because in the high-dimensional regime, where the embedding dimension is comparable to the sample size, the empirical covariance is an ill-conditioned and spectrally distorted estimator — a failure mode that random-matrix theory makes quantitative — replace it with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity, damping the unreliable small eigenvalues. The shrinkage coefficient is chosen on held-out data to stabilize the sphering operator when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening (sphering) matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing map, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against sphering that uses the unregularized covariance.

items.base: Apply VICReg's variance and covariance regularization beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. At each selected depth, the per-dimension variance hinge keeps embedding coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them, using depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding, evaluated by per-layer effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
items.padded: Treat the encoder as a cascade of information-processing stages, each of which — by a data-processing-inequality intuition — can only forward or discard, never create, the effective degrees of freedom it inherits; dimensional collapse is then the premature loss of participation ratio (effective rank) partway down the cascade. To arrest it, apply VICReg's variance and covariance penalties not only at the projector output but at several intermediate backbone stages. At each selected depth a per-coordinate variance hinge holds each dimension's spread above a floor — preserving that stage's spectral entropy — while the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates the coordinates to strip second-order redundancy, with depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The goal is to counter the collapse of effective dimensionality as it forms inside the encoder rather than only at the terminal embedding, assessed by per-layer effective rank (participation ratio) and downstream linear-probe accuracy.

items.base: During the fine-tuning stage, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay that assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. Sweep the feature-extractor learning rate and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
items.padded: Cast adaptation as a slow–fast dynamical system in the spirit of singular-perturbation theory, treating the feature extractor as a slowly relaxing manifold and the classification head as the fast variables that equilibrate on top of it. Concretely, during the fine-tuning stage assign each depth its own relaxation timescale by applying layer-wise learning-rate decay — small learning rates (low mobility) to the feature extractor and larger learning rates to the classification head — so that the pretrained features evolve quasi-adiabatically and their displacement in parameter space stays bounded. Borrowing the language of multi-objective thermodynamics, sweep the feature-extractor learning rate as a control parameter and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, tracing the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier as a Pareto front and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.

items.base: Train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by adding a regularization term that pushes the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty maintaining per-dimension variance above a floor plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.
items.padded: Reframe collapse avoidance as an information-theoretic redundancy-reduction problem in the tradition of efficient-coding theories of sensory neurons: a degenerate encoder is one whose channels carry redundant or vanishing information, so stability ought to follow from maximizing the effective coding capacity of the representation rather than from any branch asymmetry. Accordingly, train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by driving the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity — an isotropic whitening of the code: a hinge penalty that holds each channel's variance above a floor (guarding against informational collapse) plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty that decorrelates the channels to remove redundancy. The invariance term and this decorrelation regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.

items.base: Replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent to meet its own per-dimension target entropy, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions. The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms. This lets the temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.
items.padded: Draw an explicit analogy to multi-thermostat molecular dynamics, in which each degree of freedom is coupled to its own heat bath rather than to a single global temperature, so that equipartition can be enforced mode by mode. In that spirit, replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension — one thermostat per degree of freedom. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent, acting as a feedback thermostat that drives its dimension to its own per-dimension target-entropy setpoint, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions (the equipartition limit). The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms, letting the local temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.

items.base: Extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient. The coefficient interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.
items.padded: Borrow the Hurwicz criterion from decision theory under ambiguity, which grades an agent's stance between full pessimism (maximin) and neutrality through a single coefficient of pessimism, and read the two critics as an ambiguity set over value estimates. Under this lens, extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient of pessimism. The coefficient interpolates between the ambiguity-averse hard minimum and the ambiguity-neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.

items.base: Replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.
items.padded: Recast trust-region sizing as a closed-loop regulation problem in the sense of classical cybernetics — a servomechanism, like a Watt governor, that holds a plant at its setpoint despite disturbances — where the plant is the policy update and the regulated quantity is its step size in the information geometry of the policy manifold. Concretely, replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy — a distance in policy space — is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target, exactly as an error-driven governor opens or closes to track its setpoint. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.

items.base: Introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. Estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty at each state using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. The value lower-bound and the overall training objective are unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage.
items.padded: Borrowing from the physics of inhomogeneous media, promote the conservative Q-learning penalty coefficient from a global constant to a spatially varying coupling field defined across the state manifold, analogous to a position-dependent potential in a disordered material. At each state, gauge the local degree of 'disorder' through epistemic uncertainty, read off either from the fluctuation-style disagreement of an ensemble of value functions or from a kernel-density estimate of how densely the offline dataset populates that region of state space. In the spirit of screening arguments and adaptive-mesh refinement, scale the term that suppresses out-of-distribution action-values in direct proportion to this local uncertainty, so that data-rich, well-screened regions experience a weak coupling while data-poor, bare regions experience a strong one. The variational value lower-bound and the overall training objective remain exactly as before; only the single global conservatism constant is promoted to an inhomogeneous per-state field indexed by dataset coverage.

items.base: Fit the overoptimization scaling-law form of Gao et al. online during PPO training, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. Use this estimate to set and adjust the KL penalty coefficient with a feedback controller, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and allocate a larger KL allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Evaluate against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
items.padded: Cast reward-model overoptimization as a tipping-point phenomenon in a driven dynamical system: as the policy is pushed along the KL 'reaction coordinate,' the gold objective traces an inverted-U whose apex is a critical point beyond which the system decays, a Goodhart-style bifurcation. Treat the empirical overoptimization curve of Gao et al. as an equation of state and fit its functional form online during PPO from a small held-out panel of gold-labeled prompts, yielding a running estimate of the critical KL coordinate at which projected gold reward is maximized. Close a homeostatic feedback loop around this estimate, in the manner of biological set-point regulation, that continuously tunes the KL penalty coefficient so the trajectory relaxes toward the predicted critical point rather than toward a fixed KL budget, and grant a larger KL 'energy' allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted critical point lies farther out. Benchmark against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.

items.base: Extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs, and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble. Normalize this averaged error with a running estimate of its standard deviation, and linearly anneal the intrinsic-reward coefficient toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method is otherwise identical to standard RND and adds two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule.
items.padded: Recast the prediction-error novelty signal as a noisy measurement problem in the language of estimation theory and statistical physics. A single random-feature 'probe' — a predictor regressed onto a fixed, randomly initialized target embedding — furnishes just one high-variance sample of a state's surprisal. Deploy instead a replica ensemble of K independently initialized probe–target pairs and define the intrinsic bonus as the ensemble-mean prediction error, invoking the canonical variance-reduction argument (the estimator's fluctuations shrink like one over the square root of K) to obtain a cleaner novelty measurement. Standardize this averaged surprisal against a running estimate of its own standard deviation, and impose a simulated-annealing-style cooling schedule that linearly drives the intrinsic-reward coupling to zero, so behavior migrates from exploratory diffusion toward greedy exploitation as extrinsic signal is uncovered. The procedure is otherwise identical to the single-probe baseline and introduces exactly two control parameters, the replica count and the cooling schedule.

items.base: During conditional flow matching training, weight each regression target by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients. The density is estimated with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and the weighting exponent is a hyperparameter tuned on a recall metric. The scheme is applied on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, with evaluation by recall and captured-mode counts.
items.padded: Approach mode coverage as a biodiversity-preservation problem drawn from quantitative ecology, where the goal is to keep rare 'species' — the under-populated data modes — from going locally extinct under a sampler that favors abundant ones. During conditional flow-matching training, apply an inverse-propensity reweighting to each velocity regression target, in the spirit of rarefaction corrections and importance-weighted survey estimators, scaling its contribution by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that specimens sampled from sparse, under-represented modes exert proportionally larger pull on the learned field. The local density is read off with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate over a held-out census of the data, and the reweighting exponent is a single knob tuned against a recall (mode-retention) metric. The correction rides on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the deterministic ODE integrator untouched, with success gauged by recall and the tally of captured modes.

items.base: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a standard sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, following the toy-model finding that feature importance governs how features are packed into superposition. Importance is estimated from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are less likely to be suppressed. The autoencoder is trained on residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.
items.padded: Frame sparse dictionary recovery as an optimal bit-allocation problem from rate–distortion theory, in which the L1 sparsity penalty plays the role of a uniform 'coding tax' levied on every active atom. Replace this flat tax with a variable-rate schedule — a water-filling-style allocation — in which each atom's penalty is discounted in proportion to an estimate of its importance, so that high-value channels are taxed lightly and are less likely to be quantized away, exactly as an efficient code spends more bits on the components that carry more of the signal. Importance is metered either from the variance an atom accounts for in downstream activations or from its measured contribution to the loss. The encoder–decoder is fit to residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and recovery is scored against known ground-truth generative factors in the toy regime and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.

items.base: A generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and their appearance is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
items.padded: Borrowing the vocabulary of kernel-based associative memory and signal-detection theory, we recast the discrete prefix-matching detector as a graded template-completion operator of the kind long studied in Hopfield-style attractor dynamics and in hippocampal accounts of fuzzy, content-addressable recall. Concretely, this is a generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity -- a cross-correlation-style similarity kernel replacing the delta-function of exact lookup. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads, forming a separate population of graded-completion units. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation -- a lesioning probe in the tradition of neuropsychological deficit mapping -- on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and the developmental emergence of these units is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training, treated as an order-parameter-style critical point.

items.base: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.
items.padded: Treating the network as a dynamical medium through which a factual signal propagates, we frame the study as an impulse-response, Green's-function-style probe of the sort used in linear-response analysis and in lesion-deficit mapping in systems neuroscience. We apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise -- the injected impulse -- and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability, exactly the mediated quantity familiar from formal mediation analysis and epidemiological source attribution. We then map how the peak mediating layer and token position -- the epicentre of the recovered response field -- vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.

items.base: Extend the sparse autoencoder by replacing the single global L1 sparsity coefficient with a per-feature coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency across features, and add a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting. Retain the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, then sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers to measure how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves shift at matched L0 relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline.
items.padded: Drawing on the theory of homeostatic plasticity and efficient sensory coding, we treat each dictionary atom as a coding unit governed by a firing-rate-homeostasis set point. We extend the sparse autoencoder by replacing the single global L1 sparsity coefficient with a per-feature coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency across features -- a direct analogue of homeostatic set-point regulation that balances neuronal activity -- and add a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting, in the spirit of the lateral-inhibition and redundancy-reduction long invoked to explain decorrelated codes in early visual pathways. We retain the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, then sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers to measure how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves shift at matched L0 relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline.

items.base: Modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, adding a magnitude term that the standard formulation omits. Evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. Report whether the value-norm scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.
items.padded: Viewing the transformer as a layered flow network -- a signal-flow graph whose token nodes exchange mass along attention edges, so that rollout is the series composition of per-layer transfer matrices familiar from the transfer-matrix method in statistical mechanics and from conductance-weighted transport in circuit theory -- we modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, i.e. by endowing each edge with a conductance/capacity term that the standard, purely stochastic formulation omits. We evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. We report whether the value-norm (conductance-weighted) scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.

items.base: Allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. During decoding, maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion (the entropy of its recent attention distribution). Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed attention receive larger budgets, holding the total constant. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.
items.padded: Casting cache retention as a constrained resource-allocation problem -- the cache as a conserved quantity to be partitioned like transmit power across parallel channels in the water-filling solution of information theory, or like a fixed reservoir apportioned among competing consumers in thermodynamic partitioning -- we allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. During decoding, we maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, quantified as the Shannon entropy of its recent attention distribution and read as that head's demand signal. Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions (low-entropy, low-demand) receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed, high-entropy attention receive larger budgets, holding the total conserved. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.

items.base: An online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate. The controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high and fewer when it has been low. The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively rather than being held fixed.
items.padded: Borrowing the logic of optimal foraging theory from behavioral ecology, treat each verification step as a forager deciding how long to exploit a depleting patch before paying the fixed 'travel cost' of moving on. Cast as a feedback-control problem in this cybernetic idiom, an online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate, the analogue of the patch's instantaneous intake rate. Exactly as the marginal-value theorem prescribes matching residence time to a running sense of environmental quality, the controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high (a rich patch) and fewer when it has been low (a depleted one). The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively, by this closed-loop feedback rule, rather than being held fixed.

items.base: Before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.
items.padded: Recast the launch as a classical load-balancing problem from scheduling theory in operations research: a set of jobs with heterogeneous processing times must be assigned to identical parallel machines (the streaming multiprocessors) so as to minimize makespan and the fragmentation left by partially filled work units. In the spirit of longest-processing-time list scheduling and warehouse order-batching, before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences—an entropy-reducing rearrangement of the workload—makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.

items.base: Define a width-indexed drift statistic equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence. Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.
items.padded: Viewed through the lens of finite-size scaling in statistical mechanics, the empirical neural tangent kernel plays the role of a response function whose flow away from its initial value measures the breakdown of the mean-field (infinite-width) description as one departs the thermodynamic limit. Define a width-indexed drift statistic—an order parameter for this departure—equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence. Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate, the signature of a leading 1/N finite-size correction to a self-averaging quantity. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK—the linear-response prediction of the mean-field theory—and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.

items.base: Extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.
items.padded: Read heavy-ball momentum literally as classical mechanics: a massive particle sliding through a potential landscape (the loss surface) under viscous damping, its trajectory escaping to infinity along an asymptotic ray much as a hyperbolic orbit in celestial mechanics approaches its asymptote. In this dynamical-systems idiom, extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate—the direction of the escaping trajectory—still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, which acts as the attracting terminal direction, with the momentum coefficient (the particle's inertia relative to its damping) entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient—the discretization time step and the inertia-to-friction ratio—are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.

items.base: Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count, the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.
items.padded: Frame the design choice as an engineering-economics life-cycle costing problem, of the kind used to size a capital asset whose up-front construction cost trades off against its operating cost over a long service life—capital expenditure versus operating expenditure amortized across the deployment lifespan. Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model—the operating 'metabolic' load the asset must sustain. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count—capital committed to a larger body incurs a proportionally larger lifelong operating burden—the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases, echoing how life-history theory shifts optimal adult body size under a heavier lifetime maintenance demand. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.

items.base: Extend the counterfactual biasing-cue probe into a systematic benchmark: apply a battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — that alter the model's output without appearing in its stated reasoning, and quantify unfaithfulness as the rate at which such cues flip the final answer while going unmentioned. Measure how this rate varies with model scale and task category across several model families, and report the resulting benchmark for comparison.
items.padded: Borrowing the logic of psychophysical dissociation experiments — where a covert stimulus provably drives a subject's response yet never enters their verbal report — treat the model as a black-box respondent and assemble a stimulus battery of covert perturbations: permuting the ordering of the answer options, injecting a planted suggested answer, and paraphrasing the question stem. Each perturbation is engineered to displace the emitted decision while remaining absent from the narrated justification. Framed information-theoretically as a hidden leakage channel between stimulus and response, we quantify unfaithfulness as the rate at which such covert cues flip the terminal answer without surfacing in the reasoning trace — the confabulation rate of the stated justification. We then profile how this dissociation rate scales with model capacity and stratifies across the task taxonomy over several model families, and release the resulting stimulus-response battery as a comparative instrument.

items.base: At a fixed training budget, instruction examples are selected to maximize coverage of task clusters defined in a sentence-embedding space. Instructions are embedded and clustered by similarity, and a subset is chosen so that the number of distinct clusters represented is maximized rather than sampled in proportion to cluster frequency. This coverage-maximizing selection is compared against uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute. The measured outcome is zero-shot generalization to held-out task families, isolating whether cluster coverage or example quantity better explains it.
items.padded: Cast instruction curation as a biodiversity-maximization problem in the manner of community ecology: treat each task cluster as a 'species' occupying a niche in a sentence-embedding manifold, and, under a fixed sampling budget, assemble the training pool to maximize species richness — the count of distinct niches represented — rather than sampling niches in proportion to their abundance. Instructions are embedded and agglomerated into niches by similarity, and the richness-maximizing subset is contrasted with neutral (uniform-random) sampling and with merely enlarging the census by scaling the total example count under matched compute. As in rarefaction analysis, the endpoint is out-of-sample generalization — here zero-shot transfer to held-out task families — adjudicating whether niche coverage or raw census size better accounts for it.

items.base: An extension characterizes how quickly the implicit posterior concentrates on the shared concept as a function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture. Using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions, it predicts that in-context accuracy rises faster, requiring fewer examples, when the true concept is well separated from competing concepts, and more slowly when concepts overlap. A hierarchical prior over concept families is added so related concepts share structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks. Predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with controlled concept separation.
items.padded: Recast posterior concentration as a relaxation-to-equilibrium process on a statistical manifold. Treating the concept mixture as a set of competing thermodynamic phases, we characterize how quickly the implicit posterior settles onto the shared concept as a function of the geometric separation between phases, measured by information-geometric divergences between concept-conditioned distributions. In the vocabulary of nucleation kinetics, the prediction is that in-context accuracy relaxes rapidly, needing fewer examples, when the true phase is well separated from its competitors, and exhibits critical slowing-down when phases overlap. A hierarchical prior over concept families is introduced so that kindred phases share latent structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks as shared order parameters. Predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic corpora with controlled concept separation.

items.base: Compare a fixed set of BPE granularities (varying only the merge count / vocabulary size) on a matched benchmark that spans multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages, using a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one of the arms. For each granularity, measure token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy by operand length, and downstream multilingual task scores, then report the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid. The contribution is an empirical sweep quantifying how the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal granularity settings trade off within the existing BPE framework, holding model size and training data fixed.
items.padded: Frame tokenizer granularity as a coarse-graining resolution in the spirit of the renormalization group and rate-distortion theory: the merge count sets the scale at which the symbol stream is compressed, and we ask how observables flow as this scale is swept. Holding model size and training corpus fixed, we sweep a fixed ladder of BPE resolutions (varying only the merge count / vocabulary size) across a matched battery spanning multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource scripts, with a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one rung. At each resolution we record the per-language coding cost (token fertility per word), arithmetic accuracy stratified by operand length, and downstream multilingual scores, then report the fertility-accuracy correlation across the ladder. The contribution is an empirical scaling sweep charting how the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal coarse-graining scales trade off within the standard BPE codebook.

items.base: Extend CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection on the text side of region-category matching. For each category, ensemble multiple prompt templates and augment the label with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, averaging their CLIP text embeddings into a single category representation. During training of the region classifier, sample hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label to sharpen the region-text similarity boundary. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category text embedding.
items.padded: Reframe the category side of region-label matching through prototype theory from cognitive semantics: rather than a single lexical point, a category is rendered as a graded prototype formed from the centroid of its lexical neighborhood. For each category we ensemble multiple prompt templates and enrich the label with synonyms and superordinate (hypernym) terms harvested from a lexical ontology, then average their CLIP text embeddings — in effect taking a colimit over the small diagram of near-synonyms — into a single prototype representation. While training the region classifier, we draw hard-negative category names that are semantically adjacent in the ontology to the ground-truth label, sharpening the inter-prototype decision boundary as in contrastive discrimination among confusable concepts. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category prototype.

items.base: Extend PairNorm so that its single global rescaling constant is replaced by a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth. Instead of holding one target for total pairwise distance across all nodes, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, with a small number of learned coefficients fit during training. The aggregation operator is otherwise unchanged. Because high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, allowing their normalization scale to differ from low-degree nodes is intended to keep the anti-collapse effect better matched across the degree distribution as depth increases.
items.padded: Borrowing the language of the renormalization group and of heat diffusion on disordered media, treat each propagation step as a coarse-graining move along a scale axis — with network depth playing the role of diffusion time — that drives the node ensemble toward a thermodynamic fixed point, a low-frequency condensate. In this statistical-mechanics picture a site's local coordination number, i.e. its degree, sets its local relaxation rate, so a single system-wide temperature-like rescaling cannot hold the ensemble away from the condensate uniformly across sites. Accordingly, replace the normalization layer's single global rescaling constant with a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth: rather than pinning one target for the total pairwise squared distance across all nodes, set the normalization target per degree bucket and per scale (layer), governed by a small number of learned coefficients fit during training, while leaving the aggregation (diffusion) operator itself untouched. Because high-coordination sites relax toward the low-frequency fixed point faster under the Laplacian dynamics, granting them a scale-dependent normalization distinct from that of low-degree sites is meant to keep the anti-condensation effect matched across the full degree distribution as coarse-graining depth grows.

items.base: Augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors — for example, counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Because these descriptors take different values on some node pairs that 1-WL colors identically, the augmented model can distinguish certain pairs of graphs that standard message passing cannot. The proposal is to specify which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families, and to measure the resulting gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks.
items.padded: Drawing on chemical graph theory and the motif-census tradition of systems biology — where molecules and biological interaction networks are fingerprinted by tallying local topological substructures — decorate each node of a Graph Isomorphism Network with a fixed vector of precomputed structural descriptors before any message passing begins. Concretely, these are counts of small subgraph motifs such as triangles and short cycles (the graph-theoretic analogue of ring-and-clique indices in cheminformatics), together with geodesic shortest-path distance encodings measured to a chosen set of anchor landmarks. Because such topological fingerprints take distinct values on certain node pairs that the color-refinement procedure renders chromatically identical, the descriptor-augmented model resolves pairs of graphs that plain neighborhood message passing conflates. The program is to characterize, for named families of graphs, exactly which descriptor sets provably raise resolving power beyond the first-order (1-WL) refinement floor, and to quantify the resulting improvements on standard graph-classification benchmarks.

items.base: Extend DP-SGD by replacing the single global clipping norm with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each layer to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a total (ε, δ). The allocation redistributes a fixed noise budget toward layers with larger gradients, aiming to reduce clipping-induced utility loss while preserving the same overall privacy guarantee as standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.
items.padded: Recast differentially private training as the problem of conveying a gradient signal across a bank of parallel Gaussian channels — one channel per network layer, each corrupted by additive noise — in the spirit of the water-filling power-allocation results of classical information theory. Viewed this way, the single global clipping norm acts as a uniform per-channel gain, which is wasteful when the channels carry signals of very different magnitude. The proposal replaces it with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm; the additive Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each channel (layer) to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a single total (ε, δ). As in allocating a fixed transmit-power budget across subcarriers, this redistributes a fixed noise budget toward the high-magnitude layers, aiming to lower the clipping-induced distortion (utility loss) while holding the overall privacy guarantee identical to standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.

items.base: Run LiRA against image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer's strength (e.g., weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting, measure the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, and fit the resulting dose-response relationship between regularization strength and attack success. Report the functional form and identify the strength at which marginal privacy gains diminish, providing calibrated guidance for choosing regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
items.padded: Treat regularization as a therapeutic dose and privacy leakage as the pharmacodynamic response, importing the exposure-response methodology of pharmacology and toxicology. Administer the Likelihood Ratio Attack to image classifiers trained across a graded titration of a single regularizer — a weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate serving as the 'dose' — while holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed as the controlled assay conditions. At each dose, read out the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates as the response endpoint, recorded alongside the train-test generalization gap as a covariate, and fit the resulting dose-response curve linking regularization strength to attack success. The aim is to report its functional form — the analogue of a Hill-type saturation curve — and to locate the point of diminishing marginal effect (a privacy analogue of an EC-style threshold), yielding calibrated guidance for selecting regularization hyperparameters against a target attack-success budget, much as a therapeutic window is chosen in dose-finding.

items.base: Instantiate the X-learner with a single shared-trunk neural network. One representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations, and the whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages.
items.padded: Frame the estimation of individual treatment effects as reconstructing an unobservable counterfactual twin — the outcome the same unit would exhibit in a parallel world where its assignment were flipped — and cast the covariate-balancing step in the language of optimal transport and domain adaptation, where treated and control populations are two shifted distributions to be aligned. Concretely, instantiate the X-learner atop a single shared-trunk network: one learned representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads; and the two effect estimates are reconciled through an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty — an optimal-transport-flavored discrepancy between the two representation distributions — pulls the treated and control embeddings into alignment, and the entire apparatus is trained end-to-end rather than as the separate stages of the classical recipe.

items.base: Reduce the dependence of the double machine learning estimator on the particular random cross-fitting partition by computing the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregating the point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. Adjust the variance estimator to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals account for fold-assignment uncertainty. Estimate the nuisance functions on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory remain those of the original framework.
items.padded: Viewed through the lens of disordered statistical systems, each random cross-fitting partition plays the role of a single quenched realization of disorder, and any one orthogonal-score estimate is a sample drawn at fixed disorder. Following the logic of quenched self-averaging in spin-glass theory, we suppress the sensitivity of the double machine learning estimator to the particular fold assignment by drawing many independent partitions—independent disorder realizations—and combining their point estimates with a median-of-means combiner, a robust ensemble average tolerant to heavy-tailed sample-to-sample fluctuations. We then correct the variance estimator to include the between-partition (disorder) fluctuation term on top of the usual within-partition sandwich contribution, so that the reported intervals reflect the fold-assignment disorder rather than assuming perfect self-averaging. The nuisance functions on each fold are fit with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, the Neyman-orthogonal moment, and the asymptotic theory remain exactly those of the original framework.

items.base: We replace the single channel-shared linear map of the DLinear baseline with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group is given its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are kept. The method adds a clustering step and group-specific parameters relative to the shared-weight baseline. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
items.padded: We recast the shared linear forecasting map of the DLinear baseline as a problem in network community structure. Treating the empirical cross-channel correlation matrix as a weighted graph over channels, we partition the channels into communities by correlation-based clustering—modules of mutually coupled series, analogous to functional guilds in an ecological interaction network—and endow each community with its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, in place of the single channel-shared map. In condensed-matter terms this imposes a block-diagonal coupling structure on the linear operator, with intra-block weights free and inter-block weights suppressed. The moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output map are retained unchanged. Relative to the shared-weight baseline the method adds only a clustering (community-detection) step and the group-specific parameters. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.

items.base: A Chronos-style tokenized forecaster is extended to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, producing covariate tokens that are interleaved with target tokens along the sequence, with channel-identifying information added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series that carry aligned covariates, leaving the tokenizer and next-token objective unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.
items.padded: Borrowing the vocabulary of symbolic sequence biology, a Chronos-style forecaster already renders a real-valued series as a string over a fixed alphabet of quantization bins—much as a genome is a string over a fixed nucleotide alphabet. We extend this symbolic representation to carry parallel annotation tracks: each exogenous covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, yielding covariate letters that are interleaved with target letters along a single linear sequence, with channel-identifying marks added to the embeddings to distinguish the tracks, as one would tag co-aligned annotation lanes over a common genomic coordinate. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series carrying aligned covariates, while the tokenizer (the alphabet) and the next-token transcription objective are left unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.

items.base: First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.
items.padded: Framed as a question of metaplasticity—the biological regulation of how plastic each part of a system is, rather than of the plastic changes themselves—we let each layer of the network carry its own inner-loop plasticity coefficient, analogous to a layer-specific adaptive control gain or a neuromodulatory setpoint that governs the local propensity to learn. First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with these per-layer inner-loop learning rates, which are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss—learning not only where to start but how susceptible to change each layer should be. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size (gain) parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.

items.base: Combine EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty with a small experience-replay buffer in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and can also be used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.
items.padded: Cast in the language of information geometry and statistical mechanics, EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty is a harmonic (elastic) potential that anchors the parameter particle to past-task minima along the directions of the Fisher information metric—the natural Riemannian curvature tensor on the parameter manifold—while an experience-replay buffer acts as a finite reservoir of past microstates coupled into the dynamics. We combine the two in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer (a Monte-Carlo draw from the reservoir) while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and is also used to re-estimate the Fisher metric (the local curvature of the information landscape) online, rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the elastic regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.

items.base: Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility.
items.padded: Borrowing the vocabulary of frustrated systems in statistical mechanics -- where mutually competing local constraints cannot be simultaneously minimized and the ground state settles at a nonzero residual frustration energy -- treat the three fairness constraints as incompatible order parameters of a frustrated Hamiltonian. Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance, reading each epsilon as the slack a constraint is granted before it breaks. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy -- a constraint-frustration phase diagram whose boundary plays the role of a coexistence surface -- extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier, i.e. the phase boundary, on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a conserved-budget, resource-partitioning view of the incompatibility in which tightening one constraint necessarily transfers frustration into another.

items.base: Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.
items.padded: Cast prediction as sampling from a two-level disordered ensemble in the sense of statistical mechanics, with a quenched degree of freedom (the random initialization, frozen once each network is trained) sitting above an annealed degree of freedom (the dropout mask, resampled freely at inference, as in the quenched-versus-annealed disorder hierarchy of spin systems). Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks -- a partition-function-style average taken over the quenched configurations and, for each, a Monte-Carlo integration over annealed mask realizations. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations -- equivalently, how to partition a fixed sampling budget between quenched and annealed disorder -- that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.

items.base: Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.padded: Cast the observed-interaction problem as an acceptance-and-efficiency correction of the kind used to unfold detector-biased event samples in experimental particle physics, where each recorded event is up-weighted by the inverse of its detection efficiency to recover the true underlying rate; here the detector is the exposure mechanism and its efficiency is the propensity of observation. Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item -- a flat, position-independent efficiency -- with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features, that is, an efficiency map that varies across the phase space of users. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, the familiar stabilization of inverse-efficiency weights against small-denominator blow-up, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns -- their own local detector efficiency -- rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.

items.base: A connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens; a learned scalar gate weights the two streams before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.
items.padded: Taking a cue from the two-stream organization of biological vision -- a low-bandwidth pathway carrying a compressed, gist-level summary of a scene alongside a high-bandwidth pathway that preserves fine retinotopic, spatially resolved detail -- build a connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens (the summary channel) and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens (the spatially preserved channel); a learned scalar gate, analogous to a neuromodulatory mixing weight that arbitrates between the two afferent streams, weights the two before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.

items.base: Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.padded: Read the supernet's edge as a competitive neural circuit in which candidate operations vie for representation, and recall that -- as in cortical circuits stripped of gain control -- units with systematically larger output amplitude come to dominate regardless of intrinsic usefulness. Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. Borrowing the homeostatic, divisive-normalization principle by which biological neurons rescale their responses onto a common dynamic range, the proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution -- a thermodynamic temperature term that keeps the competition hot and postpones symmetry-breaking -- to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.

items.base: Extend GPTQ's Hessian-guided quantization by combining it with the outlier-channel protection from LLM.int8: identify the highest-magnitude activation channels from calibration statistics, keep the corresponding weight columns in higher precision, and run GPTQ's second-order error compensation over the remaining 4-bit columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude selects which channels to protect, and the protected fraction is tuned to trade off model size against accuracy. This yields a mixed-precision GPTQ variant where the error-compensation update accounts for the already-fixed high-precision columns.
items.padded: Cast low-bit weight compression as a lossy source-coding problem in the Shannon rate-distortion sense: the calibration set is an empirical source, the bit-width is the coding rate, and the layer's second-order curvature supplies the distortion metric on weight space (a Fisher-information-like geometry). As in strongly-correlated physical systems, the activation statistics are heavy-tailed, with a sparse subset of feature coordinates concentrating most of the spectral mass. The scheme pins these dominant coordinates, i.e. the highest-magnitude activation channels identified from calibration statistics, to a high-precision reservoir, holding their weight columns out of the aggressive 4-bit regime, and then relaxes the remaining columns toward a constrained minimum-distortion configuration using second-order (Hessian-guided) error compensation that is conditioned on the already-pinned high-precision columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude acts as the order parameter deciding which coordinates enter the reservoir, and the pinned fraction is swept to trace the resulting rate-distortion (size-versus-accuracy) frontier.
