Mechanically this is GPTQ's greedy second-order error compensation operating over a fixed high-precision channel subset in the manner of LLM.int8, re-expressed in the vocabulary of rate-distortion coding and constrained energy minimization.

items.base: At a fixed parameter budget, rank structural groups (convolutional channels and attention heads) by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics — the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning — and remove the lowest-scoring groups. Compare this against the same structured procedure using aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm) as the score. Both variants use iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluate at matched sparsity levels, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.
items.padded: Borrow the framing of activity-dependent synaptic pruning in developing nervous systems, where whole functional units are eliminated according to a use-it-or-lose-it selection signal rather than by the strength of individual connections. Treat each structural group — a convolutional channel or an attention head — as such a functional unit, a circuit module competing for survival at a fixed parameter budget, and assign it a fitness score by aggregating movement-pruning statistics over its constituent weights, namely the summed tendency of those weights to drift away from zero (to grow) during fine-tuning, read as a developmental trajectory of reinforcement. The lowest-fitness modules are culled. As a control that swaps the activity-dependent selection signal for a purely static one, the identical group-level culling is instead driven by aggregated weight magnitude (the group's L2 norm). Both regimes use iterative elimination with rewinding of the survivors to their original initialization, echoing a critical-period reset. At matched sparsity (matched culled fraction) the survivors are evaluated for task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.

items.base: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix (for example, its stable rank or spectral energy) computed once before training, instead of applying a single uniform rank to all adapted layers. Ranks are rounded to meet the budget, while the scaling factor and the rest of the LoRA parameterization are unchanged. The resulting allocation is compared against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
items.padded: View the fixed adapter-parameter budget as a total channel capacity to be distributed across a bank of parallel subchannels, exactly the allocation problem solved by water-filling in communication theory, where more capacity is poured into the subchannels that carry more usable signal energy. Here each adapted layer is a subchannel, and its usable energy is read off once, before training, from a cheap spectral statistic of its frozen pretrained weight matrix — its stable rank or spectral energy, interpretable as an effective dimensionality or participation ratio in the sense of localization physics. Each layer's LoRA rank is then allocated in proportion to that statistic rather than held uniform across layers, with ranks rounded to respect the total budget, while the scaling factor and the remainder of the low-rank parameterization are left untouched. This energy-proportional (water-filling-style) allocation is benchmarked against uniform-rank baselines at matched total parameter counts.

items.base: Extend forward loss correction by re-estimating the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each re-estimation step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are identified from the current model, and the transition matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is then computed with this running matrix. The approach keeps the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective, changing only the schedule of transition-matrix estimation and its stabilization across updates.
items.padded: Model the corrupted labels as the output of a discrete memoryless channel whose confusion (transition) matrix maps a transmitted class i to a received class j — precisely the class-conditional noise matrix of loss correction. Rather than estimating this channel once during a warm-up and assuming it is time-invariant, treat it as a slowly drifting channel and track it online with a decision-directed scheme borrowed from adaptive equalization: at fixed intervals the model's own high-confidence per-class decisions serve as pilot (anchor) symbols from which a fresh transition-matrix estimate is formed, and the running channel estimate is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates, i.e. a first-order recursive filter with a forgetting factor. The forward-corrected loss — the network's predictions composed with the transition matrix before the loss — is then evaluated with this tracked matrix. The class-conditional channel model and the forward-correction objective are unchanged; only the estimation is made recursive and stabilized across updates.

items.base: The energy score is computed as a single scalar from the logits and compared against a global threshold. Because different classes exhibit systematically different energy magnitudes, the method estimates, for each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class. At test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for OOD detection. The correction requires only stored per-class statistics and no retraining or auxiliary outlier data.
items.padded: Treat the scalar energy read from the logits as a measured observable subject to a class-dependent systematic offset, the way a metrologist treats a gauge whose zero-point shifts between instrument settings, or an astronomer who must standardize raw luminosities into comparable standard candles before they can be placed on a common scale. Because each class exhibits a systematically different characteristic energy magnitude, the method calibrates a per-class reference distribution from the in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class, storing that class's mean and standard deviation of the energy. At test time the observable is converted to a z-score against the reference of its predicted class — subtract that class's mean, divide by its standard deviation — and an out-of-distribution decision is made by comparing the standardized value to a single control limit, in the spirit of a statistical-process-control chart. Only the stored per-class summary statistics are required; there is no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data.
